coredata.dc:title: Polyoxometalates as promising enzyme mimics for the sensitive detection of hydrogen peroxide by fluorometric method
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Rui
Zhang, Boyu
Zhao, Mingming
Ma, Qiang
Qi, Yanfei
coredata.dc:description: Polyoxometalates (POMs) have been proven to possess intrinsic peroxidase-like activity. However, by far, most of POMs with intrinsic peroxidase-like activity are stable at acid aqueous. Herein, we firstly utilized Na10[α-SiW9O34] with the peroxidase-like property in basic pH condition to build a CdTe quantum dots (QDs)-based fluorometric method for detection of hydrogen peroxide. In the CdTe QDs stable pH value, Na10[α-SiW9O34] can catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into·OH radicals that could efficiently quench the fluorescence of CdTe QDs. The limit of detection in this system were 3.8 nmol/L. The RSD of detection were range from 0.88% to 2.78%. And the linear range of H2O2 is from 7.8 × 10−9 to 2.5 × 10−7 mol/L. The rate of recovery of sample addition was between 93.99% and 110.02%. The results not only built a simple, sensitive and easy handing assay but also enlarge the POMs’ analytic application field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyoxometalates
Peroxidase-like
Fluorometric method
CdTe QDs
H2O2

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of MPA-capped CdTe quantum dots and Na10[α-SiW9O34]
Na10[α-SiW9O34] and/or H2O2 on the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs
Measure of the reactive hydroxyl radicals·OH production
The effect of Na10[α-SiW9O34] on the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs
CdTe QDs fluorescence quenching by H2O2 with the Na10[α-SiW9O34] as catalyst
Determination of H2O2 in water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The effects of Na10[α-SiW9O34] (POM) on the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs. (a) The fluorescence spectra and (b) the relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs and the incubation time in the presence of Na10[α-SiW9O34].

The quenching effects of H2O2 in the absence and presence of Na10[α-SiW9O34] (POM). Conditions: Na10[α-SiW9O34] 1 × 10−3 mol/L; H2O2, 5 × 10−5 mol/L; pH, 8.0; temperature, 25◦C; incubation time, 5 min.

(a) The reaction between·OH and TA. (b) Fluorescence spectra of phosphate solutions containing only TA (A), TA and Na10[α-SiW9O34] (B), TA and H2O2 (C), or TA, H2O2 and Na10[α-SiW9O34] with different concentration((D) 0.5 mM, (E) 1 mM, (F) 2 mM, (G) 4 mM). Conditions: TA, 5 mmol/L; H2O2, 100 mmol/L; pH, 8.0; temperature, 55 °C; incubation time, 30 min.

The effects of Na10[α-SiW9O34] (POM) concentrations (0–10 mM) on the fluorescence of CdTe QDs in the absence and presence of H2O2. Conditions: H2O2, 5 × 10−5 mol/L; pH, 8.0; temperature, 25◦C; incubation time, 5 min.

The effects of pH on the fluorescence of CdTe QDs-Na10[α-SiW9O34] (POM) in the absence and presence of H2O2. Conditions: Na10[α-SiW9O34] 1 × 10−3 mol/L, H2O2, 5 × 10−5 mol/L; temperature, 25◦C; incubation time, 5 min.

The effect of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs and CdTe QDs-Na10[α-SiW9O34] (POM) in the presence of H2O2. Conditions: Na10[α-SiW9O34] 1 × 10−3 mol/L, H2O2, 5 × 10−5 mol/L; temperature, 25◦C; pH, 8.0.
Linear calibration plot for H2O2. The inset shows dependence of the absorbance on the concentration of H2O2 in the range from 0 to 1 μM.
Schematic illustration of the fluorescence quenching of CdTe QDs by H2O2 with Na10[α-SiW9O34] as catalyst.
Comparison of different fluorescent systems for the detection of H2O2.
The interference study for the determination of H2O2 ( 2 ×10−7 mol/L) by the proposed method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: △I˭I0-I, where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs-Na10[α-SiW9O34]-H2O2 system in absence and presence of interfering species.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of the analyses of H2O2 in water samples.

head.ce:title: Polyoxometalates as promising enzyme mimics for the sensitive detection of hydrogen peroxide by fluorometric method

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Public Health, Jilin University, Changchun, Jilin 130021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Public Health, Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polyoxometalates (POMs) have been proven to possess intrinsic peroxidase-like activity. However, by far, most of POMs with intrinsic peroxidase-like activity are stable at acid aqueous. Herein, we firstly utilized Na10[α-SiW9O34] with the peroxidase-like property in basic pH condition to build a CdTe quantum dots (QDs)-based fluorometric method for detection of hydrogen peroxide. In the CdTe QDs stable pH value, Na10[α-SiW9O34] can catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into·OH radicals that could efficiently quench the fluorescence of CdTe QDs. The limit of detection in this system were 3.8 nmol/L. The RSD of detection were range from 0.88% to 2.78%. And the linear range of H2O2 is from 7.8 × 10−9 to 2.5 × 10−7 mol/L. The rate of recovery of sample addition was between 93.99% and 110.02%. The results not only built a simple, sensitive and easy handing assay but also enlarge the POMs’ analytic application field.

A fluorescence quenching method for sensitive detection of hydrogen peroxide of CdTe QDs with Na10[α-SiW9O34] as catalyst. Under the optimal reaction conditions, a linear correlation was established between fluorescence intensity ratio I0/I and concentration of H2O2 from 7.8 × 10−9 to 2.5 × 10−7 mol/L with a detection limit of 3.8 × 10−9 mol/L.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is a necessary oxidant and plays an important role in food production, biomedicine, pharmaceutical, industrial and environmental applications [1]. It was also proved to be a byproduct in the oxidation metabolic process, which is responsible for the immediately dangerous to life and health when its concentration reaches 75 ppm [2]. Therefore, the quantitative determination of H2O2 is significant not only in the industrial quality control and processing applications but also in the clinic diagnosis [3,4]. The classical methods for measuring hydrogen peroxide concentrations are through direct measurement of the absorbance at 240 nm of the H2O2 molecule, or through reaction of the peroxide with ferrous iron, monitored via a subsequent reaction with the dye xylenol orange [5]. However, the two methods is not sensitive enough or prone to interferences. A number of other assays have been developed to measure hydrogen peroxide levels, such as fluorescence [3], chemiluminescence [6], liquid chromatography [7] and electrochemistry [8,9]. Among these methods, fluorometric method based on horseradish peroxidase (HRP) has been developed for the H2O2 determination [10]. And it is pushed forward by substituting horseradish peroxidase with novel artificial enzymes, such as multiferroic BiFeO3 and Fe3O4 nanoparticles [11,12]. These functional nanomaterials have exhibit unexpected enzyme-like activities, large surface-to-volume ratio and highly stability.

Polyoxometalates (POMs) are a large family of inorganic metal-oxide cluster compounds with many remarkable physical, chemical properties and biological activities [13,14]. These complexes have shown potential applications in catalysis, functional materials, magnetochemistry, analytic chemistry and medicine [15–17]. POMs has a fast and reversible multi-electron redox process without any significant structural changes that make it as redox sensors, electrochromic or photochromic materials [18–20]. It is reported that POMs has showed intrinsic enzyme activity in colorimetric assay of cancer cells, H2O2 and glucose [21–28]. For example, the tetranuclear ZrIV-substituted POMs modified by various O-donor ligands show peroxidase-like catalyst for the colorimetric detection of H2O2 [24]. Furthermore, POMs have been immobilized and hybrid with different materials to improve their properties and potential practical applications. For instance, the dipeptide- POMs-graphene oxide ternary hybrid prepared by a precipitation method show a higher peroxidase activity [28]. Considering the enzyme properties of POMs, the new fluorometric methods based on them for H2O2 can be subsequently developed.

In the design of fluorometric methods, CdTe QD was chosen as the signal source because of its unique size-dependent optical and electronic properties [29], such as tunable particle size, wide excitation spectrum, narrow and tunable emission spectra, high quantum yield and high bleaching threshold [30]. The luminescence property of QDs has been observed to be strongly dependent upon their local environments, including pH value [31]. For instance, MPA capped-CdTe QDs used in this work are stable at basic pH condition. Meanwhile, the enzyme activities of POMs are strongly pH-dependence. Most of the POMs are stable and show high enzyme activities at acid condition (about pH value 3 or 4) [22–24,28] except the folate-functionalized POM nanoparticles (at neutral pH) [21,27]. However, the folate-functionalized POM nanoparticles facilitate the fast oxidation of organic dyes without an oxidant (e.g·H2O2). Thus, the nanoparticles is useless for the detecting H2O2. Recently, we screened the enzyme activity of POMs and found that a classic trivacant Keggin Na10[α-SiW9O34] salt had peroxidase activity at basic condition [32]. Here, we design a new fluorometric method for H2O2 detection using Na10[α-SiW9O34] in QDs-based system. The fluorescence of CdTe QDs is quenched by H2O2 more efficiently and quickly in the presence of Na10[α-SiW9O34].

All the chemicals used were of analytical grade without further purification Na2WO4·2H2O was obtained from Guoyao Chemical Research Institute (Shenyang, China). Na2SiO3 was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). HCl, Na2CO3, NaCl, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), etc., were purchased from Beijing Chemical Works (Beijing, China). The purified water were obtained from SMART-N Heal Force Water purification System (Shanghai Canrex Analytic Instrument Co., Ltd Pudong Shanghai China). The resistivity of water is 18 MΩ cm. of water is 18 MΩ cm.

The fluorescence measurements were performed on a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC fluorescence spectrofluorophotometer (Kyoto, Japan). A 1 cm path length quartz cuvette was used in the experiment. The widths of the excitation and emission slits were set to 5.0 and 5.0 nm, respectively. The Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded in the range 400 − 4000 cm−1 on KBr. The pH measurements were performed by a PHS-25 pH meter (Shanghai INESA Scientific Instrument Co., China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MPA-capped CdTe quantum dots and Na10[α-SiW9O34]
ce:section.ce:para: CdTe QDs stabilized by mercaptopropionic acid were synthesized by refluxing routes as described in detail in the literature [33]. Na10[α-SiW9O34] was prepared according to literature method [34]. Briefly, 0.133 mol Na2WO4·2H2O and 12.5 mmol Na2SiO3 are dissolved in 200 mL distilled hot water, and then the HCl (6 M, 32.5 mL) was slowly added in the above solution and with vigorous stirring 30 min. The mixture was boiled to a volume of 75 mL and then collected the filtrate. Then, 3.2 mol Na2CO3 solution was slowly added into the filtrate and with stirring. The precipitate was collected after 1 h. The solid was stirred with 250 mL, 4 M NaCl and then dried under vacuum. The characteristic peaks of Na10[α-SiW9O34] (KBr pellet, cm−1) were 987, 936, 869, 836, 705, 655, which are nearly consistent with the reported in the literature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Na10[α-SiW9O34] and/or H2O2 on the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the effect of Na10[α-SiW9O34] and/or H2O2 on the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs, the mixture of CdTe QDs, Na10[α-SiW9O34] and/or H2O2 was diluted to 1 mL with PBS (pH 8.0, 20 mmol/L) and detected immediately after adding Na10[α-SiW9O34] or H2O2. The fluorescence emission wavelength of CdTe QDs used in this study is 637 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measure of the reactive hydroxyl radicals·OH production
ce:section.ce:para: The hydroxyl radicals (·OH) production was measured by fluorescence method. The terephthalic acid was used as a fluorescence probe. 100 μL 5 mmol/L terephthalic acid, 50 μL 100 mM H2O2, and 100 μL Na10[α-SiW9O34] with different concentrations (0.5–4 mM) were incubated in 750 μL PBS buffer (20 mM, pH=8.0) at 55◦C for 30 min. Then, the solution were studied for fluorometric measurement. Fluorescence spectra were obtained with excitation wavelength of 315 nm and the emission spectra were recorded in the wavelength of 425 nm.

100 μL CdTe QDs, 100 μL 1 mmol/L Na10[α-SiW9O34], 800 μL 20 mmol/L PBS buffer (pH=8.0) were mixed at room temperature. A series concentration of H2O2 were added into the mixture, sequentially. After the addition of H2O2, the fluorescence of above solution was measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer (λex= 350 nm, λem= 637 nm). The process of the fluorescence quenching of CdTe QDs by H2O2 with Na10[α-SiW9O34] catalyst is shown in Scheme 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of Na10[α-SiW9O34] on the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the phosphate-tungstic and silicotungstic acid (H3PW12O40 and H4SiW12O40) with peroxidase activity were added into the CdTe QDs aqueous solution. Then, the fluorescence of CdTe QDs was quenched immediately. The reason is that POMs (strong acids) destroy the stability of colloidal quantum dots. Considering the stable pH value of CdTe QDs and POM, the Na10[α-SiW9O34] was added into CdTe QDs aqueous solution. The lacunary-Keggin polyoxotungstates Na10[α-SiW9O34] consists of a central SiO4 tetrahedron surrounded by three W3O9 groups formed by edge-sharing octahedrons, as shown in Scheme 1. These groups are connected to each other by corner-sharing oxygen atoms. The Na10[α-SiW9O34] solution is metastable at all pH values. The fluorescence spectrum was recorded within 1 min after the addition of Na10[α-SiW9O34] into CdTe QDs solution with vigorous mixing. As shown in Fig. 1., the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs had a moderate decrease, and reached the platform after 60 s. The result indicated that Na10[α-SiW9O34] complex could not cause a further decrease in fluorescence intensity in the following experiment. The fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs-Na10[α-SiW9O34] solution was relatively stable during the detection period for H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CdTe QDs fluorescence quenching by H2O2 with the Na10[α-SiW9O34] as catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The quenching effect of H2O2 on the fluorescence of CdTe QDs was investigated in the presence and absence of Na10[α-SiW9O34] after the addition of H2O2 aqueous solution, as shown in Fig. 2. The results show that the fluorescence intensity ratio I0/I (I0 and I refer to the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs in the absence/presence of H2O2) increased slowly with the increasing of incubation time in the absence of Na10[α-SiW9O34]. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity ratio I0/I (I0 and I refer to the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs-Na10[α-SiW9O34] in the absence/presence of H2O2) increased rapidly with the increasing of incubation time in the presence of Na10[α-SiW9O34], and the fluorescence intensity reached the platform after 5 min. The fluorescence quenching extent of CdTe QDs–Na10[α-SiW9O34] system caused by H2O2 was obviously greater than that of CdTe QDs system, which indicated that H2O2 quenched the fluorescence of CdTe QDs more efficiently in the presence of Na10[α-SiW9O34]. The result was also shown that Na10[α-SiW9O34] has the similar effect to that of HRP, BiFeO3 and Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Generally, the catalytic mechanism of peroxidase can be sorted into two kinds. One is the electron transfer process, and the other is the generation of reactive hydroxyl radicals (·OH) [35]. In order to clarify the related mechanism of Na10[α-SiW9O34], the fluorescence experiments are carried out to detect·OH during the reaction. Terephthalic acid (TA) was used as fluorescence probe for tracking of (·OH) because it could capture (·OH) and generate remarkable fluorescent product, 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (HTA), at 425 nm ( Fig. 3a). In the Fig. 3b, it is clearly shown that photoluminescence (PL) intensity gradually increased with increasing of concentration of Na10[α-SiW9O34]. In addition, no PL intensity was observed in the absence of H2O2. This results revealed that Na10[α-SiW9O34] could catalytically activate H2O2 decomposition to generate·OH radicals and lead to the more efficient oxidation of CdTe QDs surface. Finally, the fluorescence of CdTe QDs is quenched.

In order to achieve the best performance, we investigated the effect of Na10[α-SiW9O34] concentrations (0.1 − 10 mM) on the fluorescence of CdTe QDs in the absence and presence of H2O2. As shown in Fig. 4a, the fluorescence intensities were both firstly decreased and then slowly increased with the Na10[α-SiW9O34] concentration increasing in the absence and presence of H2O2. As shown in Fig. 4a, the maximum fluorescence intensity difference between the system with or without H2O2 is obtained with 1 mM Na10[α-SiW9O34] solution. However, the background influence will be increasing with the increasing of Na10[α-SiW9O34] level. As shown in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity ratio I0/I firstly increased and then decreased with the increasing of the Na10[α-SiW9O34] concentrations both in the absence and presence of H2O2. When the concentration of Na10[α-SiW9O34] reached 2 mM, the effect of Na10[α-SiW9O34] on the fluorescence intensity ratio of CdTe QDs was greater than that in the presence of H2O2. Thus, aiming to obtain the better catalytic activity of Na10[α-SiW9O34] and lower background influence, 1 mM Na10[α-SiW9O34] was selected as the optimal concentration.

The MPA-capped CdTe quantum dots and Na10[α-SiW9O34] solution are stable at the pH value above 6 and metastable at all pH values, respectively. To investigate the influence of pH on the fluorescence quenching of QDs-Na10[α-SiW9O34]-H2O2 system, the fluorescence intensities were examined in the buffer solutions with various pH value (5.0–10.5). As shown in Fig. 5a, the fluorescence intensities of CdTe QDs-Na10[α-SiW9O34] in the presence of H2O2 increased from pH 5.0–9.0 and then slowly increased above pH 9.0. In all pH condition, the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs-Na10[α-SiW9O34] was greater decreased with H2O2 than that without H2O2. The maximum fluorescence intensity difference between the system with or without H2O2 is obtained at pH value 8.0 buffer solution. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 5b, the fluorescence intensity ratio I0/I (I0 and I refer to the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs-Na10[α-SiW9O34] in the absence and presence of H2O2) reached maximum at pH 8.0, which indicated that the pH 8.0 was the optimal pH in the quenching effect of H2O2 on the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs-Na10[α-SiW9O34] system.

Fig. 6 showed effects of incubation time (0–300 s) on the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs and CdTe QDs-Na10[α-SiW9O34] in the presence of H2O2. When the H2O2 were added into CdTe QDs and CdTe QDs-Na10[α-SiW9O34] aqueous solution, the fluorescence intensity has little changes from 60 to 300 s except for a little decrease from 0 to 60 s, indicating the CdTe QDs-H2O2 and CdTe QDs-Na10[α-SiW9O34]-H2O2 solution were stable during this period. Thus, 5 min was chosen as the optimal incubation time.

Under the optimized reaction condition, the effect of H2O2 concentrations on the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs-Na10[α-SiW9O34] system was investigated. As shown in Fig. 7, the fluorescence intensity ratio I0/I (I0 and I refer to the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs-Na10[α-SiW9O34] in the absence and presence of H2O2) linear increased with the increase of the concentration of H2O2. The linear regression equation is as follow: I0/I = 1.06 + 0.33 × 106 × CH2O2 with correlation coefficient of 0.98266, the liner range is from 7.8× 10−9 to 2.5× 10−7 M. The lower limit of detection (LOD) of the Na10[α-SiW9O34] for H2O2 was found to be 3.8× 10−9 M. The inset of Fig. 7 shows that the correlation between the fluorescence intensity ratio I0/I and H2O2 concentration in the range of 0–1 μM. Generally, the fluorescence quenching/enhancing sensing systems based on nanomaterials for H2O2 can be sorted into two kinds. One is the electron transfer process, and the other is the generation of reactive hydroxyl radicals (·OH). For example, Molaabasi et al. reported a label-free fluorescent detection system based on blue-emitting gold nanoclusters capped by hemoglobin (Hb-AuNCs). In the presence of hydrogen peroxide, an intramolecular electron transfer to Hb-AuNCs takes place under heme degradation and/or iron release from Hb which is accompanied by the fluorescence enhancement of the nanoclusters [36]. The HRP or CuO nanoparticles/5 aminosalicylic acid (5-ASA)/H2O2 systems is the generation of reactive radicals with the HRP and CuO as catalysis. Compared with HRP, mimic enzymes with advantages of highly stable against denaturing, low-cost, easy store and treatment might become attractive and promising candidates. Our system firstly utilized the mimic enzymes activity of polyoxometalates in fluorometric sensing H2O2. Comparing with these systems for the determination of H2O2 as shown in Table 1, the proposed method is superior in low detection limit.

For further evaluating the detection selectivity of CdTe QDs-Na10[α-SiW9O34] system for H2O2 determination, the investigation was carried out at a H2O2 concentration of 2 × 10−7 mol/L with various coexistence substrates added and the results are listed in Table 2. It is clear that all of these substances except for Ni2+, CO3 2-, Cu2+, Ag+, and citric acid have no obvious interference. Furthermore, the Ni2+, CO3 2-, Cu2+, Ag+, and citric acid are not existed in the human plasma sample. Therefore, these ions will not affect during the clinic detection of H2O2. Thus, the proposed fluorometric method displays a high selectivity for the determination of H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of H2O2 in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the feasibility of the proposed method, we test content of H2O2 in water samples under the optimum conditions. The results are listed in Table 3, it can be seen that the data of recovery is between 93.99% and 110.02%. From Table 3, it can be seen that the results obtained by CdTe QDs-Na10[α-SiW9O34] system for H2O2 determination were acceptable and suitable. The RSD of detection were range from 0.88% to 2.78%.

In summary, a new POM-based fluorescence quenching method for detection of hydrogen peroxide has been established. The result indicates that Na10[α-SiW9O34] possesses intrinsic peroxidase-like activity and its catalysis is still strong at basic pH·H2O2 can be decomposed into·OH radicals in the presence of Na10[α-SiW9O34] that could quench the fluorescence of CdTe QDs more rapidly and efficiently. Under the optimal reaction conditions, a linear correlation was established between fluorescence intensity ratio I0/I and concentration of H2O2 from 7.8 × 10−9 to 2.5 × 10−7 mol/L with a detection limit of 3.8 × 10−9 mol/L. Future research may focus on the attempting to explore the novel POM nanomaterials or change the florescence substrates in the detecting systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by NSFC (81402719) and Norman Bethune Program of Jilin University (2015228).
